nativeRange.habitat: Rocky intertidal and subtidal zones on wave-exposed and sheltered coasts
nativeRange.biotope: Temperate rocky shore; encrusts rocks from the mid-intertidal zone into the subtidal

imageCandidates.notes: Product photo from Mosaic Macros listing; sole trade source for this species.

summary: Irish Moss (Chondrus crispus) is a North Atlantic red alga — not a true moss — best known commercially as the primary source of carrageenan. It requires cool water between 6 and 18°C and is generally incompatible with tropical reef aquariums.
careNotes:
Irish Moss (Chondrus crispus) is a temperate North Atlantic red alga belonging to the family Gigartinaceae — not a true moss, despite the common name. It displays a distinctive thin, lobed, fan-branching thallus (the algal body equivalent of a stem-and-leaf structure) that tends to appear deep red to purple, with frond tips showing a characteristic bluish iridescence under full-spectrum lighting.

CRITICAL TEMPERATURE NOTE: Irish Moss is a cold-water organism native to the North Atlantic, where temperatures typically range 6–18°C (43–64°F). It has been reported to decline in tanks exceeding approximately 24°C (75°F) and is not suited for typical tropical reef aquariums, which generally run 24–27°C. Successful keeping requires either a dedicated cold-water or temperate marine setup, or a chilled system. This cold-water requirement is the primary reason Irish Moss is considered an advanced species — it is incompatible with most standard home reef aquariums.

In the wild, Irish Moss grows on rocky intertidal and subtidal substrates along Atlantic coasts of Europe and North America. It is the primary commercial source of carrageenan (up to 55% dry weight), a gel-forming compound widely used as a food and cosmetic thickener. In aquarium settings, it attaches to live rock or rubble via its holdfast. Nutrient uptake capacity is considered low relative to refugium workhorses such as Chaetomorpha, so it is kept primarily as a display specimen rather than as a filtration aid.

Propagation is typically accomplished by fragmentation — cutting sections of the thallus and allowing them to attach to rock. The species undergoes an alternation of generations (gametophyte and sporophyte phases) in nature, but captive propagation is generally limited to simple cutting. Irish Moss will MELT at the base if buried in sand; attach it to rock instead using superglue gel, zip-tie, fishing line, or by wedging it into a crevice.
breedingNotes: In nature, Irish Moss reproduces via an alternation of generations in which the gametophyte and sporophyte phases are morphologically similar and difficult to distinguish by eye. In captivity, propagation is typically accomplished by fragmentation — cutting sections of the thallus and allowing them to attach to rock or rubble until a new holdfast develops. Sexual reproduction has not been documented in home aquarium settings.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source found for this species. Confirms species name, cold-water temperature sensitivity (declines above 75°F), and display use. Limited husbandry detail provided.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Chondrus crispus
additional.notes: Cross-verified taxonomy (Gigartinaceae, Gigartinales, Florideophyceae, Rhodophyta), native range (North Atlantic — Ireland, Iceland, Faroe Islands, Baltic to Spain, Atlantic Canada/US), morphology (up to ~20 cm, 2–15 mm wide branches), habitat (rocky intertidal/subtidal), and carrageenan composition. No aquarium-specific parameters; water parameters and care data extrapolated from wild ecology and temperature warning from Mosaic.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
